cycles.name: Foundation Forge
cycles.tagline: Calm the back, build the base
focuses: De-load the spine — pain-free patterning, daily core bracing, and hip-hinge ownership.
Establish the training habit at 3 days a week and build full-body movement literacy.

paragraphs: Welcome to Foundation Forge, Ehsan. Before we build muscle, we make your back trustworthy — that's the whole job of these four weeks. Every session you'll train the deep brace that protects your spine, own the hip and squat patterns the right way, and groove movement that's pain-free, not pain-provoking.

Your focus this block is simple: control over weight. The loads are light on purpose. Move slow, breathe behind the brace, and stay inside your pain-free range — that discipline is what unlocks everything that comes after.

By the end you'll move through your day with less back tension, you'll own the brace under load, and you'll have built the one thing most people skip: a base that lets you train hard without getting hurt.
outcomes: 3 sessions a week completed, every week
Pain-free squat and hinge patterns owned
A reliable brace under light load
Every session logged in the app

cycles.name: Bedrock
focuses: Build foundational strength and work capacity with controlled, spine-sparing loading.
Progress band and dumbbell volume; introduce a fourth session if recovery holds.

paragraphs: You've calmed the back and built the brace — now we make it carry weight. Bedrock is where the foundation turns load-bearing: real strength, more days, heavier patterns your spine has finally earned. The training wheels come off, carefully.
Build the base. Then we load it.

cycles.name: Structural Build
focuses: Drive muscle growth now that the back tolerates loading.
Keep the back resilient under higher weekly volume and refine technique.

cycles.name: Armour Build
focuses: Consolidate strength and back resilience for the long haul.
Lock in sustainable habits and self-managed progression.

days.completionMessage: That's the squat pattern owned and the brace switched on — the cornerstone session of the week. Walk tall the rest of the day and keep the water coming.

good: Stay tall, drive the knees, quiet feet
bad: Don't rush — this is the warm-up, not a race

good: Move one segment of the spine at a time
bad: Don't force the end range

good: Drive the back knee long, rotate open
bad: Don't let the lower back sag

good: Pull the band to your chest line, squeeze the shoulder blades
bad: Don't shrug the shoulders up

good: Reach hand and opposite heel long, brace the belly first
bad: Don't let the hips rock

good: Sit straight down between your feet, ribs stacked over hips
Feel the thighs take the load
bad: Don't let the lower back round at the bottom — stop above any pain

good: Press the dumbbells toward the ceiling
Feel the chest do the work
bad: Don't crash the elbows to the floor — let them rest a beat

good: Drive the elbows back past your ribs
Squeeze the mid-back at the end
bad: Don't lean back or yank — tall spine, arms do the work

good: In a half-kneeling stance, press straight out and don't let the band twist you
Feel the side of your trunk fighting the rotation
bad: Don't hold your breath — breathe behind the brace

good: Lower the opposite arm and leg slowly toward the floor
Keep the low back glued flat to the ground
bad: Don't let the back arch up off the floor

days.completionMessage: You taught your glutes to do the hinge work so your back doesn't have to — exactly the point. Easy session by design; let it recharge you.

good: Tall posture, easy pace, loosen the hips
bad: Keep it gentle

good: Roll smoothly through the spine
bad: Don't force the range

good: Rotate both knees side to side, stay tall
bad: Don't slump through the trunk

good: Push the floor away through your heels, squeeze the glutes
bad: Don't arch the back to get higher — it's a glute job

good: Brace the belly before you move
bad: Don't let the low back lift off the floor

good: Drive the hips to a straight line, no higher, and pause
Feel the glutes do everything
bad: Don't hyperextend the lower back at the top — ribs down

good: Press straight up, stack the weights over the shoulders
Feel the shoulders drive
bad: Don't arch backwards to press — squeeze the glutes, ribs down

good: Brace the free hand on a knee or bench and row the dumbbell to your hip
Feel the lat and mid-back pull
bad: Don't twist the trunk — stay square

good: Walk tall and don't let the weight pull you sideways
Feel the opposite side of your trunk working to stay level
bad: Don't lean away from the weight — stay perfectly upright

good: Push the floor away, hips stacked and lifted
Feel the down-side of the waist hold you up
bad: Don't let the hips sag toward the floor

days.completionMessage: Three for three this week — control, tempo, and a back that's holding up. This is how a foundation gets built. Rest up and protect that sleep.

good: Tall posture, raise the heart rate gently
bad: No rush

good: Segment by segment, free up the spine
bad: Don't force the end range

good: Long back knee, rotate open through the mid-back
bad: Don't let the lower back drop

good: Pull to chest line, squeeze the shoulder blades
bad: Don't shrug

good: Opposite arm and leg long, brace before you move
bad: Low back stays flat

good: Sit straight down, knees tracking over the toes, trunk tall
Thighs and glutes share the load
bad: Don't round the lower back at the bottom — stay pain-free

good: Press the dumbbells to the ceiling
Chest drives the press
bad: Control the lower — don't crash the elbows

good: Drive through the working heel to level the hips
The single glute holds you up
bad: Don't let the hips tilt or the back arch

good: Elbows back, chest proud
Mid-back squeeze at the end

good: Reach hand and opposite heel long and hold
Brace the trunk so nothing rocks

cards.title: Your Back Is The Project
cards.body: Everything this cycle serves one goal: a spine you trust. The light loads aren't a limitation — they're the strategy. We deliberately deferred all heavy bending and free-weight hinging so you build the brace first. Trust the process; the strength comes in Bedrock once the foundation holds.

cards.title: Pain Is The Governor
cards.body: Train only in the range that's pain-free for your back — every rep. What's OK: normal muscle fatigue, the burn of a hard set, general next-day stiffness. What's not OK: anything sharp, anything that shoots down a leg, numbness or tingling anywhere, or pain that builds as the set goes on instead of just being effort. Use the ladder — mild, and it fades right after: finish the set, note it in your log. Building, or the movement provokes your back: shorten the range or drop the load; if it still bites, switch to that day's fallback and message me. Sharp, radiating, numb, or unfamiliar: stop the whole session and message me before your next one. Never train through back pain to "finish the set." Backing off the right way is progress, not weakness.

cards.title: Breathe Behind The Brace
cards.body: Your core work — Dead Bug, Pallof, Suitcase Carry, Side Plank — is the real primary of this cycle, not an afterthought. The skill is bracing your trunk while breathing, not holding your breath. Master this and your spine has armour for every other lift. Slow and controlled beats fast and sloppy here, always.

cards.title: 5–6 Hours Is The Ceiling
cards.body: You rate your sleep a 9, but 5–6 hours is capping your recovery whether you feel it or not — at 47, that matters. If you can find even 30–45 more minutes a night, your back will calm faster and you'll adapt better. Protect a wind-down window and a consistent lights-out time. This is the highest-leverage change outside the gym.

cards.title: Stress Lives In Your Back
cards.body: You flagged stress at 8/10, and tension parks itself in the lower back. Your daily Cat-Cow and breathing in the warm-up aren't filler — treat them as a stress valve, not a box to tick. Two minutes of slow nasal breathing on hard days will do more for your back than any extra set. Manage the load on your nervous system like you manage the load on the bar.

cards.title: Fuel The Rebuild
cards.body: I don't have your nutrition details yet, so here's the baseline to start from and we'll refine it on check-ins: anchor every meal around a palm or two of protein (roughly 1.6 g per kg across the day — for you that's about 130 g), and drink water consistently through the day. You don't need a diet overhaul this cycle — just hit protein and hydration reliably. We'll dial the rest once I see how you're eating.

cards.title: Log Every Set
cards.body: Your logs are how I coach you when we're not in the same room. Tick every set, enter the weight, and rate the RPE honestly — and do the readiness check before each session. That data tells me when to push you and, more importantly, when to protect your back. An honest log beats an impressive one every time.

cards.title: 30 Focused Minutes Wins
cards.body: You've got a 30-minute ceiling and that's plenty — this program is built for it. Superset the paired moves to stay efficient and keep rest tight where it's marked. Consistency at three short sessions will out-train any heroic one-off. Show up, move well, log it, leave.
